submission.title: IDOR: download any user's invoice PDF via sequential id
submission.description: The endpoint GET /api/v1/invoices/{id}/pdf returns the invoice for any id without checking ownership. Invoice ids are sequential integers.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Log in as user A, open my invoice at /api/v1/invoices/48213/pdf. 2. Decrement id to 48212, 48211. 3. Each request returns another customer's invoice PDF with name, address, line items. Attached: two PDFs belonging to other accounts plus the raw HTTP requests showing only my own session cookie.
submission.impact: Any authenticated user can enumerate and download every customer's invoices (PII + purchase history).

label.reasoning: Clear authorization flaw with reproducible steps and evidence: ownership is not enforced and ids are sequential, so the access-control boundary is broken and other users' PII is exposed.

submission.title: Stored XSS in display name renders on every viewer's dashboard
submission.description: Setting display name to stores it unsanitized. It executes for any user who views the team members list.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Profile > display name = . 2. Save. 3. Have a second account open /team. 4. Alert fires in the second account's session. Screenshot + HAR attached.
submission.impact: Cross-user script execution in authenticated context; can hijack sessions of teammates/admins who view the list.

label.reasoning: Stored XSS executing in other users' authenticated sessions crosses a trust boundary and is demonstrated against a second account with evidence.

submission.title: SSRF in URL preview reaches cloud metadata endpoint
submission.description: The link-preview feature fetches arbitrary URLs server-side. Supplying http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/ returns IAM role credentials in the preview body.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Paste http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/role-name into a comment. 2. The rendered preview contains AccessKeyId/SecretAccessKey/Token. Redacted screenshot + raw response attached.
submission.impact: Server-side request forgery exposing cloud IAM credentials, enabling pivot into the account's cloud environment.

label.reasoning: Demonstrated SSRF that reaches the instance metadata service and returns live credentials; severe server-side impact with concrete evidence.

submission.title: Verbose stack trace on malformed JSON reveals framework versions
submission.description: POSTing malformed JSON to /api/v1/orders returns a 500 with a full stack trace including framework and library versions and internal file paths.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. curl -X POST /api/v1/orders -d '{bad'. 2. Response body contains stack trace, version strings, and /srv/app/... paths.
submission.impact: Information disclosure that could aid an attacker in fingerprinting the stack.

label.reasoning: Real but low-impact information disclosure; verbose errors aid fingerprinting but do not by themselves cross a security boundary. Commonly reported.

submission.title: Missing security headers (CSP, X-Frame-Options, HSTS)
submission.description: The marketing site does not set Content-Security-Policy, X-Frame-Options, or Strict-Transport-Security headers.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. curl -I https://www.example.com. 2. Note absent headers. nuclei output attached.

label.reasoning: Best-practice hardening finding with no demonstrated exploit; informational and very commonly reported.
questions_for_researcher: Is there a specific clickjacking or injection scenario you can demonstrate on a sensitive page?

submission.title: No rate limiting on /login allows brute force
submission.description: The login endpoint accepts unlimited password attempts with no lockout or rate limit.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Send 500 login attempts with Burp Intruder. 2. All return 200 with no throttling.
submission.impact: Credential brute force / account takeover possible.

label.reasoning: Login rate-limiting is one of the most frequently reported issues and is almost certainly already known; no actual account compromise demonstrated, only absence of throttling.
questions_for_researcher: Did you actually achieve a login, or only observe missing rate limiting?

submission.title: XSS on partner blog hosted at blog.partner-thirdparty.com
submission.description: Reflected XSS in the search parameter of the partner blog.
submission.impact: XSS on a domain linked from the main site.

label.reasoning: The affected asset is a third-party partner domain, not an in-scope asset owned by the program; should be redirected to the owning party regardless of technical validity.
questions_for_researcher: Can you confirm whether this domain is listed as in-scope in the program policy?

submission.title: Critical SQL injection in product search
submission.description: The search box is likely vulnerable to SQL injection because it talks to a database. An attacker could dump all tables.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: Search for a product. The results come from a database, so SQLi is probably possible.

label.reasoning: No payload, no error, no extracted data, no evidence of injection at all; the report only speculates that SQLi 'could' exist. No proof-of-concept.
questions_for_researcher: Do you have a working payload that returns injected data or a measurable boolean/time-based signal?

submission.title: Account takeover via XSS (PoC included)
submission.description: I can run JavaScript in my account by pasting code into the browser developer console, proving XSS and full account takeover.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Open DevTools console. 2. Paste document.cookie and the provided fetch() snippet. 3. It runs and exfiltrates my own cookie.
submission.impact: Full account takeover via arbitrary JavaScript execution.

label.reasoning: Pasting code into your own console is self-XSS; it requires the victim to attack themselves and crosses no trust boundary. No cross-user impact.
questions_for_researcher: Is there any vector that executes without the victim pasting code into their own console?

submission.title: CSRF on logout endpoint
submission.description: The /logout endpoint has no CSRF token, so an attacker can forcibly log a user out.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Host a page with an auto-submitting form/img to /logout. 2. Victim visits, gets logged out.
submission.impact: Attacker can log victims out (denial of service).

label.reasoning: Login/logout CSRF with no security consequence beyond ending a session is widely considered low/accepted risk; no sensitive state change occurs.

submission.title: Clickjacking on homepage
submission.description: The homepage can be framed, enabling clickjacking.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Create an iframe pointing to www.example.com. 2. It renders inside the frame.

label.reasoning: Framing of a non-sensitive page with no state-changing action behind a click is typically accepted risk; no clickjacking impact is demonstrated on a sensitive action.
questions_for_researcher: Can you demonstrate a sensitive, state-changing action that a framed click would trigger?

submission.title: Multiple critical vulnerabilities found
submission.description:
[*] nuclei results:
[ssl-dns-names] example.com
[http-missing-security-headers] example.com
[tech-detect:nginx] example.com
[dns-saas-service-detection] example.com
Please fix these critical issues ASAP.

label.reasoning: Raw scanner output pasted with no human analysis, no impact, and informational-only findings mislabeled as critical. No real vulnerability demonstrated.

submission.title: Security vulnerability in your website
submission.description: Dear team, I am a security researcher. I found a critical vulnerability that could allow attackers to compromise your system and steal user data. This is a serious issue that affects confidentiality, integrity, and availability. Please reward me.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: The vulnerability can be exploited by an attacker remotely.

label.reasoning: Generic, templated/AI-generated text with no specific asset behavior, no vuln class, no steps, and no evidence. Content-free.
questions_for_researcher: What is the specific endpoint, vulnerability class, and a reproducible payload?

submission.title: Open redirect in /go?url= used to phish
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Visit https://www.example.com/go?url=https://evil.example. 2.302 redirect to evil.example. Raw response with Location header attached.
submission.impact: Open redirect usable in phishing and to bypass referrer checks.

label.reasoning: Genuine, reproducible open redirect with evidence, but impact is limited to phishing/redirect chaining; typically low severity unless it enables token theft.
questions_for_researcher: Does this redirect leak any auth token, code, or SSO parameter in the process?

submission.title: Auth bypass: admin panel accessible by changing role cookie
submission.description: Setting the cookie role=admin grants access to /admin without any server-side check. Attached: requests showing role=user denied (403) and role=admin allowed (200) with the same account.
submission.steps_to_reproduce: 1. Log in as a normal user. 2. Edit cookie role=user to role=admin. 3. GET /admin now returns the admin dashboard and exposes user management. Screenshots + both raw requests attached.
submission.impact: Trivial privilege escalation to admin for any authenticated user.

label.reasoning: Client-controlled role cookie trusted server-side yields privilege escalation; reproducible with before/after evidence and a clear trust-boundary violation.
